Question: What did David do after defeating the Amalekites?
Response: David returned from defeating the Amalekites and remained in Ziklag for two days without receiving any news of the battle between the Philistines and Israelites.

Question: On the third day, who came to David from Saul’s camp?
Response: On the third day, a man with his clothes torn and with dirt on his head came to David.

Question: What did the young man say about Saul and Jonathan?
Response: The young man said that Saul and his son Jonathan were killed.

Question: Where did the young man say he found Saul?
Response: The young man said he was on Mount Gilboa, and Saul was there leaning on his spear.

Question: What did the young man say Saul asked him to do?
Response: The young man said Saul asked him to stand over him and kill him.

Question: Why did Saul ask the young Amalekite man to kill him?
Response: Saul asked the young Amalekite man to kill him because great agony had seized him, but his life was still in him.

Question: After the young man killed Saul, what did he take from Saul?
Response: The young man took the crown that was on Saul’s head and the band that was on his arm.

Question: What did David and his men do when they heard about the deaths of Saul and Jonathan?
Response: David and his men tore their clothes, and they mourned, wept, and fasted until evening.

Question: For whom else were David and his men mourning?
Response: David and his men also mourned for the people of Yahweh and for the house of Israel, because they had fallen by the sword.

Question: Why did David have the young Amalekite man killed?
Response: David had the young Amalekite man killed because the man’s own words testified that he had killed Yahweh’s anointed.

Question: Why did David have the young Amalekite man killed?
Response: David had the young Amalekite man killed because the man’s own words testified that he had killed Yahweh’s anointed.

Question: Where is the funeral song that David sang for Saul and Jonathan written?
Response: The song is written in the Book of Jashar.

Question: What was slain on Israel’s high places?
Response: The splendor of Israel was slain there.

Question: Why did David not want the news of the defeat proclaimed in Gath or Ashkelon?
Response: He did not want the daughters of the Philistines to rejoice or triumph.

Question: Where does the shield of the Saul, the mighty king, remain?
Response: The shield of the mighty king remains on ground on Mount Gilboa.

Question: What did Saul do for the daughters of Israel?
Response: He made Israel a safe and prosperous place in which they had beautiful scarlet clothes and expensive jewelry and gold ornaments to wear.

Question: How did David describe his love for Jonathan?
Response: David said Jonathan’s love for him was more wonderful than the love of women.

Question: What did David ask Yahweh, and what was the reply?
Response: David asked if he should go up to one of the cities of Judah, and Yahweh told him to go up.

Question: When David inquired further, to which city did Yahweh tell David to go?
Response: Yahweh told David to go to Hebron.

Question: Who went up with David to Hebron?
Response: David went up to Hebron with his two wives, Ahinoam and Abigail, and all his men and their families.

Question: What did the men of Judah do when they came to David?
Response: The men of Judah came and anointed David as king over the house of Judah.

Question: Who was Abner?
Response: Abner was the son of Ner and the commander of Saul’s army.

Question: What did Abner do with Ishbosheth, the son of Saul?
Response: Abner took Ishbosheth to Mahanaim and made him king of all the territory that would later be the northern kingdom of Israel.

Question: How long was David king in Hebron over the house of Judah?
Response: David was king in Hebron over the house of Judah for seven years and six months.

Question: Where did Joab’s men and Abner’s men meet?
Response: They met near the large pool of Gibeon, staying on opposite sides of the pool.

Question: How many young men fought before Joab and Abner?
Response: Twelve men from Benjamin for Ishbosheth and twelve men from David’s soldiers fought.

Question: Who won the battle that day?
Response: The soldiers of David defeated Abner and the soldiers from the other tribes of Israel.

Question: Which son of Zeruiah pursued Abner?
Response: Asahel pursued Abner.

Question: How did Abner try to convince Asahel to stop pursuing him?
Response: Abner warned Asahel that if he had to strike him down, he could never again have a peaceful relationship with Asahel’s brother Joab.

Question: What happened to Asahel when he refused to stop pursuing Abner?
Response: Abner struck him in the stomach with the butt end of his spear, and the spear went right through his stomach and came out of his back, so Asahel fell and died there.

Question: To what location did Joab and Abishai pursue Abner?
Response: Joab and Abishai pursued Abner to the hill of Ammah, which is near Giah on the way to the wilderness of Gibeon.

Question: What did Abner say to stop Joab from pursuing him?
Response: Abner called out to Joab that they should not continue this deadly battle any longer. He told Joab to realize that if they continue fighting, many people will suffer great harm. He said that they are all Israelites, and asked Joab to please tell his soldiers right now to stop chasing Abner and his soldiers.

Question: Where did Abner and his men travel?
Response: Abner and his men marched back to Mahanaim.

Question: Where did Joab and his men travel?
Response: Joab and his men traveled to Hebron to bury Asahel’s body in the tomb of his father at Bethlehem. Then they marched all night, going back to Hebron, their home.

Question: In the war between the house of Saul and the house of David, who grew stronger and who grew weaker?
Response: The house of David grew stronger, and the house of Saul grew weaker.

Question: Who were David’s first three sons born to him in Hebron?
Response: Amnon, Chileab, and Absalom were the first three sons born to David in Hebron.

Question: What did Ishbosheth accuse Abner of doing?
Response: Ishbosheth accused Abner of doing something wrong by having sexual relations with his father Saul’s concubine.

Question: After Ishbosheth’s accusation, what did Abner swear he would do?
Response: Abner swore that he would transfer the kingdom from Saul’s house to David and establish David’s throne over all Israel and Judah.

Question: What was David’s one condition for making a covenant with Abner?
Response: Abner had to bring his wife Michal, Saul’s daughter, when he came to see David.

Question: What price had David paid for Michal to be his wife?
Response: David had paid the price of 100 Philistine foreskins for Michal.

Question: What did Abner tell the elders of Israel to do?
Response: Abner told the elders to make David their king, since Yahweh spoke about David, saying that by the hand of his servant David my servant, he would save his people Israel from the hand of the Philistines and from the hand of all of their enemies.

Question: What did David do for Abner when Abner and his twenty men arrived in Hebron?
Response: David made a feast for Abner and his men.

Question: What did Abner tell David he planned to do?
Response: Abner said he would gather all of Israel to David so they could make a covenant with him, and so David could reign over all that he desired.

Question: How did David and Abner part ways?
Response: David sent Abner away, and he went in peace.

Question: What did Joab accuse Abner of doing?
Response: Joab accused Abner of coming to deceive David and to learn all about his plans.

Question: What did Joab do after he had Abner brought back to Hebron?
Response: To avenge the death of his brother Asahel, Joab took Abner aside to the middle of the gate and struck him in the stomach, and he died.

Question: Why did David curse Joab for murdering Abner?
Response: Joab and Abishai his brother had murdered Abner because he had killed Asahel their brother at Gibeon in the battle. They should not have taken revenge.

Question: After Joab killed Abner, what curse did David pronounce on Joab’s family?
Response: David pronounced a curse that the guilt for Abner’s blood would be on Joab and his father’s house forever. He asked that there will always be someone in Joab’s family who has sores or who is a leper or who cannot walk without a crutch or who dies in battle or who does not have enough food to eat.

Question: What did David tell Joab and all the people with him to do?
Response: David told them to tear their clothes, put on sackcloth, and mourn for Abner.

Question: What did David swear when the people urged him to eat?
Response: David swore an oath that he would not taste bread or anything else for the rest of this day.

Question: What did all the people of Israel understand from David’s mourning for Abner?
Response: They understood that the king had not wanted Joab to kill Abner.

Question: What did the king say to his servants about Abner?
Response: The king told his servants that a commander and a great man had fallen that day in Israel.

Question: What did David hope for the sons of Zeruiah?
Response: David hoped that Yahweh would repay the evildoer according to his evil.

Question: What caused Ishbosheth’s hands to become weak?
Response: When Ishbosheth heard that Abner was dead in Hebron, his hands became weak.

Question: What were the names of two of Ishbosheth’s commanders?
Response: The names of two of his commanders were Baanah and Rechab.

Question: What was the name of Jonathan’s son who was crippled in his feet?
Response: The name of Jonathan’s son who was crippled in his feet was Mephibosheth.

Question: How did Rechab and Baanah get into Ishbosheth’s house?
Response: They went into the middle of the house as if they were getting wheat.

Question: What did Rechab and Baanah do once they were inside Ishbosheth’s house?
Response: They went to his private bedroom and they stabbed and killed him as he was lying asleep on his bed, and then they removed his head.

Question: Where did Rechab and Baanah go after they killed Ishbosheth?
Response: They brought the head of Ishbosheth to David at Hebron.

Question: What did David say about Rechab and Baanah?
Response: David said they were wicked men who had murdered a righteous man in his own house on his bed.

Question: On David’s orders, what did the young men do to Rechab and Baanah?
Response: The young men killed Rechab and Baanah, cut off their hands and feet, and hung them beside the pool at Hebron.

Question: What did David have done with the head of Ishbosheth?
Response: David had the head of Ishbosheth buried in Abner’s grave in Hebron.

Question: What did the tribes of Israel say Yahweh had promised David?
Response: They said Yahweh had promised that David would shepherd his people Israel and become their ruler.

Question: Who came to Hebron, made a covenant with David, and anointed him king over Israel?
Response: All the elders of Israel came to Hebron, made a covenant with David, and anointed him king over Israel.

Question: How many years did David reign in Jerusalem over all Israel and Judah?
Response: David reigned in Jerusalem for 33 years over all Israel and Judah.

Question: By what other names was Jerusalem called?
Response: The stronghold of Zion was also called the City of David.

Question: From whom did David capture the stronghold of Zion?
Response: David captured the stronghold of Zion from the Jebusites.

Question: Why did David become greater and greater?
Response: David became greater and greater because Yahweh, the God of Armies, was with him.

Question: What did Hiram, king of Tyre, send to David?
Response: Hiram sent messengers, cedar trees, carpenters, and stonemasons to David to build a house for David.

Question: How many sons were born to David in Jerusalem?
Response: Eleven sons were born to David in Jerusalem.

Question: What did the Philistines do when they heard that David had been anointed king over Israel?
Response: When the Philistines heard David had been anointed king, they marched toward Jerusalem to try to capture David.

Question: What did Yahweh tell David when David asked if he should attack the Philistines?
Response: Yahweh told David to go up because he would definitely enable David’s army to defeat them.

Question: Where did David attack the Philistines, and what was the outcome?
Response: David attacked the Philistines at Baal Perazim and defeated them there.

Question: When the Philistines came up a second time, how did Yahweh tell David to attack them?
Response: Yahweh told David not to go up to attack them from the front, but to circle around behind them and attack from opposite the balsam trees.

Question: What sound did Yahweh tell David to listen for before attacking the Philistines?
Response: Yahweh told David to attack when he heard the sound of marching in the tops of the balsam trees.

Question: What was David’s response to Yahweh’s command?
Response: David did just as Yahweh had commanded him.

Question: What was the outcome of this battle against the Philistines?
Response: David’s soldiers defeated the Philistine soldiers and chased them from the city of Geba all the way west to the city of Gezer.

Question: Why did David gather all the chosen men of Israel?
Response: David gathered the chosen men of Israel to bring up the Box of God from Baalah of Judah.

Question: On what did they set the Box of God?
Response: They set the Box of God on a new cart pulled by two oxen.

Question: Who was guiding the new cart?
Response: Uzzah and Ahio, the sons of Abinadab, were guiding the new cart.

Question: What happened at the threshing floor of Nacon?
Response: The oxen stumbled, and Uzzah reached out to steady the Box of God.

Question: What did God do in response to Uzzah’s action?
Response: God became angry and struck Uzzah for his fault, and Uzzah died there by the Box of God.

Question: How did David feel about what Yahweh had done to Uzzah?
Response: David was angry because Yahweh had broken out against Uzzah. Then David became afraid of Yahweh, and he said that it was too dangerous for him to try to bring the sacred chest to where he lived in Jerusalem.

Question: What did David do with the Box of God after Uzzah died?
Response: David decided not to bring the sacred chest to the City of David. Instead, he had the people take it into the house of Obed Edom, there by the road.

Question: Why did David bring the Box of God from Obed Edom’s house to the City of David?
Response: David brought it because he was told that Yahweh had blessed the house of Obed Edom because of the Box of God.

Question: What did David do as the Box of God was being brought up?
Response: As the Box of God was being brought up, David, wearing a linen ephod, danced before Yahweh with all his strength.

Question: What was Michal’s response when she saw King David leaping and dancing before Yahweh?
Response: When Michal saw King David leaping and dancing before Yahweh, she despised him in her heart.

Question: After the Box of Yahweh was set in its place, what offerings did David make to Yahweh?
Response: David offered burnt offerings and peace offerings to Yahweh.

Question: What did David distribute to all the people?
Response: He distributed a cake of bread, a date cake, and a raisin cake to each person.

Question: What did Michal say to David when he returned home?
Response: She sarcastically asked how the king of Israel had honored himself by uncovering himself before the eyes of his servants’ female servants, like a worthless person.

Question: What was David’s response to Michal’s rebuke?
Response: David said that he celebrated before Yahweh, who chose him as leader. He said he would humble himself even more, and that the female servants would honor him.

Question: What did Yahweh give David?
Response: Yahweh gave David rest from all his surrounding enemies.

Question: What did the king say to Nathan the prophet?
Response: The king said to Nathan that he was living in a house of cedar, but the Box of God was dwelling in a tent.

Question: What did Nathan tell David before the word of Yahweh came to him?
Response: Nathan told David to do all that was in his heart, because Yahweh was with him.

Question: What question did Yahweh ask about the leaders of Israel?
Response: Yahweh asked if he had ever commanded one of the leaders of Israel to build him a house of cedars.

Question: What did Yahweh say he had done for David?
Response: Yahweh said he took David from the pasture to be ruler over Israel, had been with him everywhere, and had cut off all his enemies from before him.

Question: What did Yahweh say he was going to do for Israel?
Response: Yahweh said he had established a place for them and will plant them so they could live in their own place and not be afflicted by wicked people anymore.

Question: Who did Yahweh say would build a house for his name?
Response: Yahweh said he would raise up David’s own offspring after him, and that offspring would build a house for his name.

Question: What did Yahweh say about David’s house and kingdom?
Response: Yahweh said David’s descendants would continually rule the kingdom of Israel after him.

Question: After Nathan told David all the words Yahweh had said, what did David do?
Response: David went in and sat before Yahweh and prayed to him.

Question: After hearing the prophecy, what did David say about Yahweh?
Response: David said that Yahweh knew his servant so well.

Question: Why did David say Yahweh had done this great thing and revealed it to him?
Response: David said Yahweh had done it for the sake of his word and according to his own heart.

Question: Why did God go to redeem Israel?
Response: God redeemed Israel to make them a people for himself and to make a name for himself.

Question: Why did David say he had been bold enough to pray like this to Yahweh?
Response: David said he found the heart to pray because Yahweh had revealed that he would build a house for David.

Question: What did David say about Yahweh’s words?
Response: David said that Yahweh’s words are true.

Question: What did David ask Yahweh to do?
Response: David asked Yahweh to be pleased to bless his house so that it would continue forever before him.

Question: What did David gain by attacking and subduing the Philistines?
Response: They captured Metheg Ammah and made the Philistines subject to the Israelites.

Question: How did David decide which Moabite soldiers would live or die?
Response: He made them lie on the ground and measured them with a rope, measuring two rope lengths to be killed and one full rope length to be kept alive.

Question: What happened to the Moabites who were left alive?
Response: The rest of the Moabites had to accept David as their ruler. Every year they had to give him the payment that he demanded.

Question: What did David capture from Hadadezer?
Response: David captured from him 1,700 horsemen and 20,000 men on foot.

Question: What did David do to the Arameans of Damascus when they came to help Hadadezer?
Response: David struck 22,000 men among the Arameans.

Question: Why was David victorious?
Response: Each time David led his soldiers into battle, Yahweh enabled them to win victories over their enemies.

Question: What did Toi, king of Hamath, do when he heard that David had defeated Hadadezer’s army?
Response: Toi sent his son Joram to greet and bless David, and Joram brought vessels of silver, gold, and bronze.

Question: What did David do with the silver and gold from the nations he had subdued?
Response: David consecrated the silver and gold to Yahweh.

Question: Where did David strike the Arameans?
Response: David killed 18,000 Arameans in the Valley of Salt.

Question: What did David do for all his people?
Response: David did justice and righteousness for all his people.

Question: How did David’s sons serve his kingdom?
Response: David’s sons were his advisors.

Question: Why did David want to show covenant faithfulness to anyone left in Saul’s house?
Response: David wanted to show covenant faithfulness for the sake of Jonathan.

Question: How did Ziba answer the king when he was asked if anyone was left in Saul’s house?
Response: Ziba told the king that one son of Jonathan was still alive but not able to walk.

Question: Who was left in Saul’s house?
Response: Mephibosheth, the son of Jonathan, the son of Saul, was left.

Question: What kindness did David show Mephibosheth for Jonathan’s sake?
Response: David restored to Mephibosheth all the land of his grandfather Saul, and he had Mephibosheth eat meals with his family continually.

Question: What did David tell Ziba to do for Mephibosheth?
Response: David told Ziba that he, his sons, and his servants were to work the land for Mephibosheth and bring in the harvest.

Question: Where did Mephibosheth live after David showed him kindness?
Response: Mephibosheth lived in Jerusalem because he ate meals with David’s family in the palace.

Question: What physical problem did Mephibosheth have?
Response: Mephibosheth was lame in both of his feet.

Question: Who became king of the Ammonites after the former king died?
Response: Hanun his son became the new king.

Question: How did David show kindness to Hanun?
Response: David sent his servants to console Hanun concerning the death of his father.

Question: What did the Ammonite commanders say about David’s show of kindness to Hanun?
Response: The commanders told Hanun that David had sent his servants to explore the city in order to spy on it and overthrow it.

Question: What did Hanun do to David’s servants?
Response: He commanded his soldiers to seize David’s officials and insult them by shaving off one side of their beards and cut off the lower part of their robes so that their buttocks would show. Then he sent them back to Israel.

Question: Why did the Ammonites hire soldiers from other nations?
Response: The Ammonites hired soldiers because they saw that they had greatly insulted David.

Question: Where did the Ammonites and their hired soldiers position themselves for battle?
Response: The Ammonites formed a battle line at their city gate, while their hired soldiers from Aram, Tob, and Maacah were by themselves in the field.

Question: What did David do when he heard that the Ammonites had hired soldiers?
Response: When David heard of it, he sent Joab and all the army of mighty men to fight against them.

Question: How did Joab prepare for the battle?
Response: Joab chose some of the best men of Israel and arrayed them to meet the Arameans, and he put the rest of the people in the hand of his brother Abishai to meet the Ammonites.

Question: What did the Ammonites do when they saw that the Arameans had fled from Joab?
Response: When the Ammonites saw that the Arameans had fled, they also fled from Abishai and went into the city.

Question: What did the Arameans do when they saw they had been defeated by Israel?
Response: The Arameans gathered themselves together again at Helam, and Hadadezer sent for many troops from Aramean kingdoms beyond the Euphrates River.

Question: What did David do when he heard that the Arameans had gathered again?
Response: David gathered all Israel, crossed the Jordan, and came toward Helam to fight the Arameans.

Question: What did all the kings who were servants of Hadadezer do when they saw they were defeated by Israel?
Response: They made peace with Israel and served them. The Arameans would not help the Ammonites anymore after that, because they were afraid of Israel.

Question: When did kings normally go out to war?
Response: Kings normally went out to war at the return of the year, in the spring.

Question: Where was King David at this time?
Response: David sent out his commander Joab with his officers and the rest of the Israelite army, but David himself stayed in Jerusalem.

Question: Who did David see one evening when he was walking on the roof of his palace?
Response: David saw a very beautiful woman bathing, and her name was Bathsheba.

Question: Who was Bathsheba’s husband?
Response: Bathsheba’s husband was Uriah the Hittite.

Question: What did David do with Bathsheba?
Response: David sent for her, he had sexual relations with her, and she became pregnant.

Question: After David found out Bathsheba was pregnant, what did he do?
Response: He told Joab to send Uriah the Hittite to him.

Question: When Uriah came to David, what did David try to get him to do?
Response: David tried to get Uriah to go down to his house and relax for a while.

Question: What did Uriah do after David told him to go to his house?
Response: Uriah did not go to his house, but instead slept at the entrance of the king’s house with all the servants of his master.

Question: Why did Uriah say he would not go to his own house?
Response: Uriah said he would never go to his house to eat, drink, and lie with his wife while the Box, Israel, and Judah were dwelling in tents, and Joab and his men were camping in the open field.

Question: What did David’s letter to Joab say?
Response: The letter said to put Uriah in the front of the strongest battle and then to retreat from him so he would be struck and die.

Question: What did Joab think might happen when his messenger brought news of the war to David?
Response: Joab thought David might become angry because he had allowed the Ammonites to kill some of his soldiers.

Question: What news did Joab think David might be angry about?
Response: Joab thought David might be angry that soldiers got so close to the city to fight because the Ammonite soldiers would shoot arrows at them from the top of the city wall.

Question: What did Joab tell the messenger to say if David got angry?
Response: Joab told the messenger to say that his servant Uriah the Hittite was also dead.

Question: What did David tell the messenger to say to Joab?
Response: David told the messenger to encourage Joab by telling him to not feel badly since no one ever knows who might die in a battle. David advised him to strengthen his battle against the city and destroy it.

Question: What did Uriah’s wife do when she heard that her husband was dead?
Response: When she heard her husband was dead, she mourned for him.

Question: What happened to Bathsheba?
Response: After her mourning passed, David sent for her, and she became his wife, and she bore him a son.

Question: Who was displeased with what David had done?
Response: Yahweh was very displeased with what David had done.

Question: Why did Nathan go to David?
Response: Nathan went to David because Yahweh sent him.

Question: What was Nathan’s story about?
Response: The story was about a rich man with many flocks and herds and a poor man who had only one little ewe lamb that was like a daughter to him.

Question: What did the rich man do with the poor man’s lamb?
Response: When a traveler came to the rich man, he took the poor man’s lamb and prepared it as a meal for his guest.

Question: What was David’s reaction to Nathan’s story?
Response: David was very angry and said that he wanted to execute the man who did that.

Question: What did Nathan tell David after David reacted in anger?
Response: Nathan told David, “You are the man.”

Question: What did Yahweh say David had done?
Response: Yahweh said David had struck Uriah the Hittite with the sword, had taken his wife to be his own wife, and had murdered him with the sword of the Ammonites. Yahweh said that David did not take seriously what he commanded when he said that his people must not commit adultery or murder. David did things that Yahweh considers to be very evil!

Question: What did Yahweh say would happen because David despised him and took Uriah’s wife?
Response: Yahweh said that he would make someone from David’s own family cause disaster for him. Yahweh would take David’s wives away from him and give them to that person instead. He will have sexual relations with them in public.

Question: After he confessed his sin, what did Nathan say would happen to David?
Response: Nathan told David that Yahweh had taken away his sin and that he would not die.

Question: What did Nathan say would happen to the son born to David?
Response: Nathan said that because David had shown great contempt for Yahweh by doing this, the son born to him would surely die.

Question: What did David do when the child was very sick?
Response: David prayed to God that the child would not die, fasted, and went in and slept on the floor of his room.

Question: When did the child die?
Response: The child died on the seventh day.

Question: What did David do once he knew the child was dead?
Response: David arose from the ground, washed and anointed himself, changed his clothes, and went to the house of Yahweh and prostrated himself. Then he asked his servants for food and he ate it.

Question: Why did David stop fasting after the child died?
Response: David reasoned that there was no reason to fast since he could not bring the child back again.

Question: Why did Yahweh send word through Nathan to name the new son Jedidiah?
Response: Yahweh said to name him Jedidiah because of Yahweh.

Question: Against whom was Joab fighting at this time?
Response: Joab was fighting against Rabbah of the sons of Ammon.

Question: Why did Joab tell David to gather the rest of the army and capture the city?
Response: Joab did not want to capture the city himself, because if he did, it would renamed to honor him and not King David.

Question: What did David make the people of Rabbah do after he captured the city?
Response: David brought out the people and set them to work with saws, iron picks, and iron axes. David’s troops also forced them to make bricks.

Question: To whom was Amnon, the son of David, attracted?
Response: Amnon loved his beautiful half-sister Tamar, who was the sister of Absalom.

Question: How did Amnon respond when Jonadab asked him why he was so weak?
Response: Amnon told Jonadab that he loved Tamar, his brother Absalom’s sister.

Question: What did Amnon do to get to see Tamar?
Response: Amnon pretended to be sick, and when David came to see him, he asked David to let Tamar come and make food for him so he could eat from her hand.

Question: What did Amnon do when Tamar came to feed him?
Response: When Tamar came to feed him, he seized her and told her to come and lie with him.

Question: What did Amnon do when Tamar begged him not to force her?
Response: He refused to do what she said he should do. He was stronger than she was, so he forcibly had sexual relations with her.

Question: How did Amnon feel toward Tamar after he raped her?
Response: Amnon hated her with a very great hatred that was greater than the love with which he had loved her.

Question: What did Amnon command Tamar to do?
Response: Amnon commanded Tamar to leave.

Question: What did Amnon order his servant to do?
Response: He ordered his servant to send Tamar outside and to lock the door behind her.

Question: What did Tamar do after she was put out of Amnon’s room?
Response: Tamar tore the long robe that she was wearing. She put ashes on her head, and she put her hand on her head. As she went away, she kept screaming.

Question: What was King David’s response when he heard about these things?
Response: When King David heard all these things, it made him very angry, but he did nothing about it.

Question: How did Absalom feel toward Amnon after he raped Tamar?
Response: Absalom hated Amnon because he had humbled his sister Tamar.

Question: Whom did Absalom invite to visit him at Baal Hazor?
Response: Absalom invited the king and all the king’s sons to visit him.

Question: What did the king say in response to Absalom’s invitation?
Response: The king said they should not all go because they would be a heavy burden to Absalom.

Question: What did David say when Absalom asked him to let Amnon go with them?
Response: But the king replied, “You do not need to invite him to the celebration either.”

Question: Who went with Absalom?
Response: Absalom urged David, so he sent Amnon and all the king’s sons with him.

Question: What did Absalom command his servants to do to Amnon?
Response: Absalom commanded his servants to kill Amnon when Amnon began to be drunk with wine.

Question: What did the king’s sons do after Absalom’s servants killed Amnon?
Response: All the king’s sons arose, and they got onto their mules and escaped by riding away fast.

Question: What was the first news David heard about the incident?
Response: The first report to David said that Absalom had killed all the king’s sons and not one was still alive.

Question: What did Jonadab say had happened and why?
Response: Jonadab said that only Amnon is dead. Absalom has been determined to kill him ever since the day that Amnon raped his sister Tamar.

Question: Where did Absalom go after having Amnon killed?
Response: Absalom fled to Talmai, the son of Ammihud, king of Geshur.

Question: How long was Absalom in Geshur?
Response: Absalom was in Geshur for three years.

Question: What did King David intend to do?
Response: King David had intended to attack Absalom and punish him for killing Amnon.

Question: What did Joab do when he knew the king’s heart was against Absalom?
Response: Joab sent to Tekoa and brought a wise woman from there to act as if she had been in mourning for a long time.

Question: What did Joab want the wise woman to do?
Response: Joab wanted the wise woman to pretend to be a mourner and to go speak to the king the words Joab told her to say.

Question: When the wise woman went to the king, whom did she say she was?
Response: The wise woman told the king she was a widow.

Question: What did the wise woman tell the king her two sons had done?
Response: She told the king that her two sons fought in a field, and one struck the other and killed him.

Question: What did the wise woman tell the king she was afraid would happen to her remaining son?
Response: She said she was afraid the whole clan would kill her remaining son, destroying her heir and leaving no descendants to preserve her family’s name.

Question: Why did the wise woman ask the king to remember Yahweh his God?
Response: She asked the king to remember Yahweh so that the avenger of blood would not destroy her son.

Question: Who did the king swear by when he told the woman that not one hair of her son would fall to the ground?
Response: The king swore by the life of Yahweh.

Question: Why did the woman say the king had been doing the wrong thing?
Response: The woman said the king was acting badly because he had not brought back his own banished son.

Question: What did the woman say God does instead of taking away life?
Response: She said that God forgives people who have sinned against him so that they can once more be in a good relationship with him.

Question: Why did the woman say she came to speak to the king?
Response: She said she came because the people had frightened her, and she hoped the king would listen and deliver her.

Question: What did the woman say about the king?
Response: She said that what the king says will comfort her, because he is like an angel of God, and he knows how to determine what is right and what is wrong.

Question: What did the king ask the woman?
Response: The king asked the woman if Joab arranged for her to do all this.

Question: Why did Joab have the woman go to the king?
Response: Joab did that to try to get David to feel differently about this matter.

Question: What did Joab do when the king told him to bring Absalom back?
Response: Joab lay facedown on the ground in honor and gratitude to the king.

Question: What instructions did the king give about Absalom?
Response: The king said that Absalom could return to his own house but could go and speak with the king.

Question: What was notable about Absalom’s appearance?
Response: He was so handsome that people throughout Israel praised Absalom for being a very handsome man. He did not seem to have any flaws in any part of his body. His hair was long, thick, and heavy.

Question: How long did Absalom live in Jerusalem without seeing the king’s face?
Response: Absalom lived in Jerusalem for two years, during which David still did not allow Absalom to come and speak with him.

Question: What did Absalom tell his servants to do after Joab refused to come to him?
Response: Absalom told his servants to go and burn Joab’s barley field with fire.

Question: What did Absalom want Joab to do?
Response: Absalom wanted Joab to go to the king and ask why he had come from Geshur, and to say that he wanted to come and speak with him.

Question: What did the king do when Absalom bowed before him?
Response: The king kissed Absalom on the cheek to show that he was no longer hostile to him.

Question: What did Absalom prepare for himself?
Response: Absalom prepared a chariot and horses for himself.

Question: Who ran before Absalom’s chariot?
Response: He hired fifty men to run in front of his chariot.

Question: Whom would Absalom call to as he stood by the gate?
Response: Absalom would call to any man who had a dispute and was coming to the king for judgment.

Question: What would Absalom say about the cases of the men who came for judgment?
Response: Absalom would say to them that their matter was good and right, but the king is not able to decide everyone’s case, and he has not appointed anyone else to judge cases for people like you.

Question: Why did Absalom say he wished he were a judge in the land?
Response: Absalom said he wished he were a judge so that every person with a dispute could come to him and he would give them justice.

Question: What did Absalom do when a man approached to prostrate himself?
Response: Absalom would stretch out his hand, hold the man up, and kiss him on the cheek.

Question: What did Absalom steal from the men of Israel?
Response: Absalom stole the hearts of the men of Israel. The Israelites began to wish that Absalom were their king instead of David.

Question: What reason did Absalom give the king for needing to go to Hebron?
Response: Absalom said he had to go to Hebron to fulfill a vow he had made to Yahweh.

Question: What did the spies that Absalom sent throughout Israel say?
Response: The spies said that when people heard the sound of the shofar, they should say that Absalom has become the king at Hebron.

Question: Did the two hundred men who went with Absalom to Hebron know of his plans?
Response: No, the two hundred men came because he had invited them to the feast he was going to host there. They were not part of his conspiracy. They knew nothing about what he was planning to do.

Question: What did David tell his servants to do after a messenger told him that the hearts of the men of Israel were behind Absalom?
Response: David told his servants in Jerusalem to arise and flee, because there would be no escape from Absalom.

Question: Whom did the king leave behind in Jerusalem to keep the house?
Response: The king left ten women, who were concubines, to keep the house.

Question: Who marched with the king as he left Jerusalem?
Response: All his servants, all the Cherethites, all the Pelethites, and all the Gittites—600 men from Gath—marched with him.

Question: What did Ittai the Gittite tell the king after the king told him to return?
Response: Ittai swore that he would go wherever the king would go, and he would fight to defend David even if he had to die doing that.

Question: Which way did David and all those with him go as they left Jerusalem?
Response: Since Absalom would be coming from the southwest, the king and the others crossed the Kidron Valley and then went up the hill on the road that goes through the desolate area east of Jerusalem.

Question: What did the king tell Zadok the priest to do with the Box of God?
Response: The king told Zadok to take the Box of God back to the city.

Question: Why was David going to wait at the fords of the wilderness?
Response: He was going to wait there until a word came from the priests to tell him what to do.

Question: What did David pray when he was told that Ahithophel was with Absalom?
Response: David prayed that Yahweh would make Ahithophel give Absalom bad advice.

Question: Who did David meet at the top of the Mount of Olives?
Response: David unexpectedly met Hushai the Archite, who had torn his clothes and put dirt on his head to show that he was very sad.

Question: What did David want Hushai the Archite to do for him?
Response: David wanted Hushai to return to the city and offer to be Absalom’s servant so he would be able to counter any good advice that Ahithophel would give to Absalom.

Question: To whom did David want Hushai to report everything he heard in the king’s palace?
Response: David wanted Hushai to report everything to Zadok and Abiathar the priests.

Question: What did Ziba, the servant of Mephibosheth, bring to David?
Response: Ziba brought a pair of saddled donkeys, 200 loaves of bread, 100 clusters of raisins, 100 summer fruits, and a skin of wine.

Question: According to Ziba, why did Mephibosheth stay in Jerusalem?
Response: Ziba said Mephibosheth stayed because he said that the house of Israel would return the kingdom of his father to him that day.

Question: What did the king say now belonged to Ziba?
Response: The king told Ziba that all that belonged to Mephibosheth was now his.

Question: What did Shimei do to David and all the king’s officials?
Response: Shimei came out cursing, and he threw stones at David and all the servants of King David.

Question: What did Shimei call David?
Response: Shimei called David a man of blood and a man of wickedness.

Question: What did David hope Yahweh would do in response to Shimei’s cursing?
Response: David hoped that Yahweh would look at his affliction and return good to him in place of the cursing.

Question: When Absalom came to Jerusalem, what did Hushai say to him?
Response: Hushai said to Absalom, “May the king live! May the king live!”

Question: To whom did Hushai tell Absalom he would belong?
Response: Hushai told Absalom that he would belong to the one whom Yahweh, the people, and every man of Israel had chosen.

Question: What did Ahithophel advise Absalom to do?
Response: Ahithophel advised Absalom to go in to his father’s concubines whom David had left to keep the house.

Question: How was Ahithophel’s counsel viewed?
Response: The counsel of Ahithophel was viewed as if God himself had recommended it.

Question: What did Ahithophel advise Absalom and all the elders of Israel about how he should attack David?
Response: Ahithophel’s advice was to let him lead a large army carry out a surprise attack on David that night, when he was weary and weak, kill only David, and bring back all the rest of the people for Absalom to rule.

Question: What was Hushai’s opinion of Ahithophel’s plan?
Response: Hushai said that the counsel Ahithophel had given on this occasion was not good.

Question: Why did Hushai say the plan was not good?
Response: Hushai said that David and his very brave warriors were probably in a safe place, and David’s soldiers will certainly kill some of Ahithophel’s soldiers at the start of the battle. When people hear about that, they will say that David’s soldiers are slaughtering Absalom’s soldiers! Then your other soldiers, even if they are as fearless as lions, will become very afraid.

Question: What did Hushai advise Absalom to do?
Response: Hushai advised Absalom to gather all of Israel, from Dan to Beersheba, and for Absalom himself to go into the battle. He said that this great army would overwhelm David’s smaller army and none of them would survive.

Question: How did Absalom and the men of Israel respond to Hushai’s advice?
Response: They said the counsel of Hushai was better than the counsel of Ahithophel and they decided to do what Hushai suggested.

Question: What message did Hushai send to David?
Response: The message was that David should not stay at the place where people can walk across the Jordan River but should cross over immediately, because otherwise, Absalom and his army may come and kill them.

Question: How was information relayed to King David?
Response: A maidservant of the two priests would go and declare news to Jonathan and Ahimaaz, and they would go and declare it to King David.

Question: What did Jonathan and Ahimaaz do when a young man saw them and told Absalom?
Response: The two of them went quickly to the house of a man in Bahurim and went down into a well in his courtyard.

Question: How were the two men helped?
Response: The man’s wife spread a covering over the well and spread grain on it, and when Absalom’s servants asked where the men were, she told them they had crossed the brook.

Question: After Absalom’s men left, what did the two men do?
Response: They came up from the well, went and reported the news to King David, and told him to get all these people across the Jordan River quickly.

Question: What did Ahithophel do when he saw that his counsel was not followed?
Response: Ahithophel saddled his donkey, went to his house, gave to his family instructions about what to do with his possessions, and then he hanged himself because he knew that otherwise David would kill him as a traitor.

Question: How were David and his people cared for in the wilderness?
Response: Shobi, Machir, and Barzillai brought beds, basins, pottery, and all kinds of food for David and the people with him to eat.

Question: What did King David tell the army?
Response: King David told the army that he would also go out with them himself.

Question: What was the response from the men in the army to King David?
Response: The men told King David not to go out, because he was worth ten thousand of them, and it was better for him to be ready to help them from the city.

Question: What instructions did the king give his commanders about Absalom?
Response: The king told the commanders not to harm the young man Absalom for his sake.

Question: What was the outcome of the battle in the Forest of Ephraim?
Response: The people of Israel were struck down before the servants of David, and more of Absalom’s soldiers died because of accidents in the forest than the number of them whom David’s soldiers killed in the battle.

Question: What happened to Absalom when the mule he was riding ran under the thick branches of a large oak tree?
Response: Absalom’s head was caught in the oak tree. The mule kept going, and that left Absalom dangling in the air.

Question: Why was Joab upset with the man who saw Absalom?
Response: Joab was upset because the man did not kill Absalom immediately.

Question: Why did the man refuse to kill Absalom?
Response: He refused because he had heard the king command his commanders to keep from hurting the young man Absalom.

Question: What did Joab do to Absalom?
Response: Joab took three spears and thrust them into the heart of Absalom while he was still alive in the oak tree.

Question: What was done with Absalom’s body?
Response: They threw Absalom’s body into a great pit in the forest and set up a very great heap of stones over him.

Question: Why had Absalom set up a pillar for himself in the Valley of the King while he was alive?
Response: Absalom set up a pillar to make his name remembered, because he said he had no son to make people remember his name.

Question: Why did Joab tell Ahimaaz not to run to the king with the news?
Response: Joab told him not to bring the news that day because the king’s son was dead.

Question: Why did Ahimaaz still run and tell David the news?
Response: Ahimaaz insisted on running after the Cushite, even though Joab told him there was no reward for the news, so Joab told him to go.

Question: What did the king think when the watcher reported a lone runner?
Response: The king said that if the man was by himself, that indicates that he is bringing news.

Question: What did the king think when the watcher reported another lone runner?
Response: The king said that this one was also bringing news.

Question: What did the king think when the watcher identified the first runner as Ahimaaz?
Response: The king said that he was a good man and came with good news.

Question: What news did Ahimaaz bring to the king?
Response: Ahimaaz told the king that Yahweh had defeated the men who had lifted their hand against the king.

Question: How did Ahimaaz answer when the king asked about Absalom?
Response: Ahimaaz said he saw a great tumult but did not know what it was about.

Question: What was the Cushite’s answer when the king asked about Absalom?
Response: The Cushite said that he wished that what happened to Absalom would happen to all of David’s enemies and to anyone who rebels against him.

Question: How did the king respond to the news of his son’s death?
Response: The king trembled, went up to the upper room of the gate, and wept, wishing he had died in place of his son.

Question: Why did the victory that day turn into mourning for all the people?
Response: The victory turned into mourning because the people heard that the king was grieved because of his son.

Question: What was the king’s cry?
Response: The king cried, “My son Absalom! Absalom, my son, my son!”

Question: What did Joab say would have pleased the king?
Response: Joab said that he could see that if Absalom were still alive and all of David’s men were dead today, David would like that better.

Question: What did Joab tell the king would happen if he did not go out and speak to his servants?
Response: Joab swore by Yahweh that if the king did not go out, all his soldiers will have abandoned him by tomorrow morning.

Question: When did all the people come before the king?
Response: All the people came before the king after they were told that the king was sitting in the gate.

Question: What were the people in the tribes of Israel arguing about?
Response: The people were arguing about what they should do now that Absalom was dead and David had left Israel to escape from Absalom.

Question: Whom did King David send to speak to the elders of Judah?
Response: David sent Zadok and Abiathar the priests to speak to the elders of Judah.

Question: Whom did David say would be the commander of his army in place of Joab?
Response: David said Amasa would be the commander of the army in place of Joab.

Question: Why did the men of Judah come to Gilgal?
Response: The men of Judah came to Gilgal to meet the king and to bring him across the Jordan.

Question: Who rushed to the Jordan to meet the king?
Response: Shimei, with 1,000 men from Benjamin, and Ziba, with his 15 sons and 20 servants, rushed to the Jordan.

Question: Why did Shimei say he was the first from the house of Joseph to meet the king?
Response: Shimei came first because he knew that he had sinned against the king.

Question: What did the king say to Shimei after Abishai said Shimei should be put to death?
Response: The king told Shimei that he would not die, and he swore an oath to him.

Question: How did Mephibosheth look when he came to meet the king?
Response: Mephibosheth had not cared for his feet, trimmed his mustache, or washed his clothes from the day the king went away.

Question: What reason did Mephibosheth give for not going with the king?
Response: Mephibosheth told the king that his servant, Ziba, had deceived him and left without him, and Mephibosheth could not follow him because he was crippled.

Question: What else did Mephibosheth say Ziba had done to him?
Response: Mephibosheth said Ziba had lied about him to the king.

Question: How did Mephibosheth reply when the king said he and Ziba should divide the field?
Response: Mephibosheth told the king to let Ziba take the whole field, because all that mattered to him was that the king had returned safely.

Question: What had Barzillai done for the king?
Response: Barzillai had provided all of the food that the king and his soldiers needed while they were at Mahanaim.

Question: Why did Barzillai say he should not go to Jerusalem with the king?
Response: Barzillai said he was too old, could no longer taste what he ate or drank, could not hear singers, and would be a burden to the king.

Question: What did Barzillai request of the king?
Response: Barzillai asked to return to his own city to die, but he asked the king to take his servant Chimham and do for him what was good in the king’s eyes.

Question: Who crossed over to Gilgal with the king?
Response: Chimham, all the people of Judah, and also half the people of Israel crossed over to Gilgal with the king.

Question: Why did the men of Israel say they had more right to David than the men of Judah?
Response: The men of Israel said they had more right to David because they represented ten tribes that were related to the king. The men of Judah represented only one tribe. Also, the people of Judah had started the rebellion against David.

Question: Whose words were harsher, the men of Judah or the men of Israel?
Response: The word of the men of Judah was harsher than the word of the men of Israel.

Question: Why was Sheba called a man of wickedness?
Response: He was called a man of wickedness because he blew a shofar and told the men of Israel to return home; he said David did not belong to them at all.

Question: How was the response of the men of Judah to Sheba different from the men of Israel?
Response: The men of Judah clung to their king, but the men of Israel did what Sheba had said and returned to their homes.

Question: How did David’s relationship with his ten concubines change?
Response: He put them in another residence where someone guarded them. They had to stay in that house until they died. David provided what they needed, but he never had sexual relations with them again.

Question: What did the king tell Amasa to do?
Response: The king told Amasa to call up the men of Judah within three days and to be there himself.

Question: How did David respond when Amasa was delayed?
Response: David told Abishai to take the soldiers already here and go to capture him. If not, we will no longer know where his is going, and the delay would allow him and his soldiers to go into fortified cities.

Question: What did Joab do when he greeted Amasa?
Response: As Joab held Amasa’s beard with his right hand to kiss him, he struck him in the stomach with the sword in his left hand.

Question: What did one of Joab’s men do after Joab stabbed Amasa?
Response: He stood by Amasa and called for those who delighted in Joab and David to follow Joab, and then he removed Amasa’s body from the road into a field so the soldiers would not stop to look.

Question: What did Joab and his men do when they found Sheba in Abel Beth Maacah?
Response: They besieged him in the city built a dirt ramp up against the outer wall of the city to batter and collapse the city wall.

Question: How did the wise woman describe the residents of her city of Abel to Joab?
Response: She described the residents as peaceful and loyal Israelites.

Question: What did the woman ask Joab?
Response: The woman asked Joab why he would try to destroy an Israelite community.

Question: What condition did Joab give the woman for withdrawing from the city?
Response: Joab said that if they would give him Sheba, who is rebelling against King David, he would go away from the city.

Question: How did the people of the city respond to Joab’s condition?
Response: The woman came to the people in her wisdom, and they cut off the head of Sheba and threw it to Joab.

Question: What was Adoniram’s responsibility?
Response: Adoniram was over the forced labor.

Question: Why was there a famine in the land for three years?
Response: There was a famine because of the bloodguilt on Saul and his house. The Israelites had solemnly promised that they would not kill them, but Saul had tried to kill all of them because he wanted the people of Judah and Israel to be the only ones living in that land.

Question: Why did King David want to make atonement to the Gibeonites?
Response: David wanted to make atonement so that they ask Yahweh to do good things for us Israelites again.

Question: What did David agree to do to atone for the injustice done to the Gibeonites?
Response: David agreed to do whatever the Gibeonites asked him to do.

Question: What did the Gibeonites request from David to atone for Saul’s actions?
Response: They requested that seven men from Saul’s sons be given to them so they could hang them before Yahweh at Gibeah.

Question: How did David respond to the Gibeonites’ request?
Response: David spared Mephibosheth but gave them the two sons of Rizpah and the five sons of Michal, and the Gibeonites hanged them on the hill.

Question: What did Rizpah do to express her grief for her sons?
Response: She did not allow any birds to come near the corpses during the day, and she did not allow any animals to come near them during the night from the time that people started to harvest barley until the autumn rains began.

Question: From whom did David take the bones of Saul and Jonathan?
Response: David took the bones of Saul and Jonathan from the leaders of the city of Jabesh Gilead.

Question: How did Abishai rescue David when he was weary?
Response: When David was weary, Abishai helped him by striking and killing the Philistine giant Ishbi Benob, who had intended to kill David.

Question: Why did David’s men insist that he no longer go into battle with them?
Response: They said that if enemy soldiers killed him in battle, Israel would no longer have him as king to guide them.

Question: Whom did David and his servants kill in Gath?
Response: They killed four giants who were born to Rapha in Gath.

Question: When did David sing the song that Yahweh was his rock?
Response: David sang the song on the day Yahweh delivered him from the hand of all his enemies and from the hand of Saul.

Question: When David calls on Yahweh, what does he say that Yahweh does?
Response: David says when he calls on Yahweh, he is saved from his enemies.

Question: What did David feel like when he was surrounded by his enemies?
Response: David felt like the waves of death and the cords of Sheol surrounded him.

Question: How did David respond to his distressing circumstances?
Response: In his distress, David called to Yahweh, and Yahweh heard his voice from his temple.

Question: What happened because God was angry?
Response: The earth shook and trembled, smoke went up from his nose, and a stream of fire was flowing out from his mouth to burn up everything in its path.

Question: How did Yahweh come down from the heavens?
Response: He lowered the heavens and came down, riding on a cherub and flying on the wings of the wind.

Question: How did Yahweh scatter his enemies?
Response: Yahweh thundered from the heavens, sent out arrows and flashes of lightning, and panicked them.

Question: How were the foundations of the world uncovered?
Response: The foundations of the world were uncovered at the rebuke of Yahweh, from the blast of the breath of his nose.

Question: How did Yahweh rescue David from the strong enemy who hated him?
Response: David said it was as if Yahweh reached down from heaven and grabbed him so that he could pull David up out of the deep water of the ocean.

Question: Why did Yahweh deliver David?
Response: Yahweh delivered David because he delighted in him.

Question: How did David keep the ways of Yahweh?
Response: David kept the ways of Yahweh and did not turn away from his statutes.

Question: What happened because David was blameless and kept himself from iniquity?
Response: Yahweh gave back to him according to his righteousness and his cleanness.

Question: What does Yahweh do to the proud?
Response: Yahweh is aware of people who are arrogant, and he humiliates them.

Question: What is Yahweh to everyone who takes refuge in him?
Response: Yahweh is a shield to all who take refuge in him.

Question: Why did David say God was his rock and fortress?
Response: David said God was his rock, the only real God, because God is where he can go to be safe. He said God was his fortress of strength because, to David, God is like a very secure place where he could go to hide from danger.

Question: Why had David’s feet not slipped?
Response: David says that God gave him many ways to respond to each situation, so that his enemies were not able to trap him.

Question: What did David do to his enemies when they looked for a savior?
Response: David defeated them so completely that they were no longer any threat to him no more than tiny particles of dust would be.

Question: What did Yahweh do for David?
Response: Yahweh rescued David from the disputes of his people and kept him as the head of nations.

Question: What did Yahweh do on behalf of David?
Response: Yahweh enabled David to conquer enemies, protecting him from being captured by them. Yahweh kept David safe when people rebelled against him and rescued hm from men who tried to harm him.

Question: Why did David sing praises to the name of Yahweh?
Response: David said he thanks Yahweh so that all people groups will know that Yahweh is the one who has helped him. He sings to praise Yahweh for the great things he has done, faithfully showing kindness to David and to his descendants in the future.

Question: What are the last words of David said to be?
Response: They are the oracle of David, the man raised to a high position, the anointed of the God of Jacob, and the one who wrote beautiful songs for the people of Israel.

Question: What did the God of Israel say to David?
Response: The God of Israel said that a righteous one ruling over man in the fear of God is like the light of morning when the sun rises.

Question: Why will the wicked be like a thorn thrown away?
Response: God will remove people who disrespect him, just as people throw away thorns that hurt their hands when they try to pick them up.

Question: How did Eleazar defeat the Philistines?
Response: When other Israelites retreated in fear, Eleazar arose and struck the Philistines until his hand cramped and he could not let go of his sword, Yahweh won a great victory for the Israelites on that day.

Question: What did Shammah defend against the Philistines?
Response: When other Israelite soldiers ran away from the battle, Shammah stationed himself in the middle of the field full of ripe lentils and fought and killed the Philistines, who wanted to steal the lentils. And so Yahweh won a great victory for the Israelites.

Question: What did the three mighty warriors do when they heard David express his desire for water from the well of Bethlehem?
Response: The three warriors broke through the camp of the Philistines, drew water from the well, and brought it to David.

Question: Why did David refuse to drink the water from the well of Bethlehem?
Response: David said it would not be right to drink it because he said that would be like drinking the blood of these men who were willing to die to get it for him.

Question: For what was Abishai known?
Response: He once fought against 300 enemy soldiers and killed them all. As a result, he became famous like the three very greatest soldiers.

Question: How did Benaiah kill a huge Egyptian soldier?
Response: Benaiah went down against the Egyptian with a staff, seized the spear from the Egyptian’s hand, and killed him with his own spear.

Question: What did David ask Joab to do?
Response: David asked Joab to go through all the tribes of Israel and count the men who could fight as soldiers.

Question: Why was Joab reluctant to count the people?
Response: Joab did not understand why David wanted to count the people, and he did not think it was a good idea to count how many soldiers they had.

Question: How long did it take for Joab to go throughout all the land and return to Jerusalem?
Response: It took nine months and twenty days for Joab to go throughout all the land and return to Jerusalem.

Question: How many fighting men did Joab count?
Response: They reported to David that there were 800,000 men in Israel and 500,000 men in Judah who were fit to serve as soldiers in the army.

Question: Why did David feel very badly after he had counted the people?
Response: David realized that he had committed a very serious sin, confessed it to Yahweh, and asked Yahweh to forgive him for his foolish act.

Question: What message did Yahweh give to David through the prophet Gad?
Response: Yahweh told David that he must choose one of three punishments.

Question: What three choices of punishment did God give to David?
Response: God offered a choice of seven years of famine, three months of fleeing from his enemies, or three days of plague in the land.

Question: Why did not more than 70,000 people die in the plague?
Response: Yahweh grieved about punishing any more people, and told the angel to lower his hand.

Question: What did David request of Yahweh when he saw the angel?
Response: David said that he had sinned, and he asked that Yahweh’s hand be against him and his father’s house, not against the people.

Question: What did Gad the prophet tell David to do?
Response: Gad told David to go up and set up an altar for Yahweh on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.

Question: What was David’s purpose for going to Araunah?
Response: David wanted to buy the threshing floor to build an altar for Yahweh so that he will stop the plague that has been killing so many Israelites.

Question: Why did David insist on buying Araunah’s threshing floor for a price?
Response: David insisted on buying it because he would not offer to Yahweh burnt offerings that cost him nothing.

Question: How did David stop the plague in Israel?
Response: David built an altar for Yahweh and he offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. Then Yahweh answered David’s prayers, and he ended the plague that had been killing so many Israelites.
